title: The Last Lighthouse Keeper
summary: A man keeps a lighthouse that no ship really needs anymore.
body:
Pedro lives on a small island near Portugal. He is the keeper of an old lighthouse. Every night, he turns on the big light. The light shines over the dark sea. Pedro is seventy years old. He has done this job for forty years.

But here is the strange part. Ships do not need his light now. Modern ships use GPS. They have computers and maps on a screen. They do not look for the lighthouse. So why does Pedro stay?

Pedro smiles when people ask. "The light is not only for ships," he says. "It is for the fishermen who feel afraid in the dark. When they see my light, they feel calm. They know land is near."

Last year, the government wanted to close the lighthouse. They said it was old and useless. But the local fishermen wrote many letters. They asked the government to keep the light on. "We do not need it," one fisherman said, "but we want it."

So the light still shines. Pedro is happy. He cleans the glass every morning. He paints the white walls in summer. The lighthouse is not useful, but it is loved. Sometimes a thing can matter even when no one needs it.

annotations.meaningVi: hệ thống định vị toàn cầu

grammarNotes.title: Present simple for habits
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng thì hiện tại đơn để nói về thói quen lặp lại hằng ngày. Động từ thêm '-s' với he/she/it.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: We use present simple for daily habits, as in 'Every night, he turns on the big light.' The verb adds '-s' for he.

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for duration
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng 'has done ... for' để nói một việc bắt đầu trong quá khứ và còn tiếp tục đến nay.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: We use present perfect for something that started before and continues now: 'He has done this job for forty years.'

grammarNotes.title: But for contrast
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Từ 'but' nối hai ý trái ngược nhau trong một câu hoặc giữa các câu.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'But' joins two opposite ideas: 'The lighthouse is not useful, but it is loved.'

translationVi:
Pedro sống trên một hòn đảo nhỏ gần Bồ Đào Nha. Ông là người trông coi một ngọn hải đăng cũ. Mỗi đêm, ông bật ngọn đèn lớn. Ánh đèn chiếu sáng trên mặt biển tối. Pedro bảy mươi tuổi. Ông đã làm công việc này bốn mươi năm.

Nhưng đây là điều lạ. Bây giờ tàu thuyền không cần ánh đèn của ông nữa. Tàu hiện đại dùng GPS. Chúng có máy tính và bản đồ trên màn hình. Chúng không tìm ngọn hải đăng. Vậy tại sao Pedro vẫn ở lại?

Pedro mỉm cười khi người ta hỏi. "Ánh đèn không chỉ dành cho tàu thuyền," ông nói. "Nó dành cho những người đánh cá thấy sợ trong bóng tối. Khi họ thấy ánh đèn của tôi, họ thấy yên lòng. Họ biết đất liền ở gần."

Năm ngoái, chính phủ muốn đóng cửa ngọn hải đăng. Họ nói nó cũ và vô dụng. Nhưng những người đánh cá địa phương đã viết nhiều lá thư. Họ xin chính phủ giữ ánh đèn sáng. "Chúng tôi không cần nó," một ngư dân nói, "nhưng chúng tôi muốn có nó."

Vậy là ánh đèn vẫn sáng. Pedro hạnh phúc. Mỗi sáng ông lau kính. Mùa hè ông sơn những bức tường trắng. Ngọn hải đăng không hữu ích, nhưng nó được yêu quý. Đôi khi một thứ có thể có ý nghĩa ngay cả khi không ai cần đến nó.

options: On a small island near Portugal
In a big city in Spain
questions.explanation: The passage says he 'lives on a small island near Portugal.'
It says 'Pedro is seventy years old.'

questions.prompt: How long has Pedro done this job?
questions.explanation: 'He has done this job for forty years.'
questions.hint: Last line of the first paragraph.

questions.prompt: Why do modern ships NOT need the lighthouse?
options: They never come near the island
questions.explanation: 'Modern ships use GPS. They have computers and maps on a screen.'

questions.prompt: Who does Pedro say the light is really for?
questions.explanation: 'It is for the fishermen who feel afraid in the dark.'
questions.hint: Read what Pedro says with a smile.

questions.prompt: What did the government want to do last year?
questions.explanation: 'The government wanted to close the lighthouse.'

questions.prompt: How did the fishermen try to save the light?
In the passage, the word 'useless' is closest in meaning to:
questions.explanation: The government 'said it was old and useless,' meaning not useful.

questions.prompt: What does the fisherman mean by 'We do not need it, but we want it'?
options: They like the light even if it is not necessary
questions.explanation: It shows they value the light for feeling, not for use.
questions.hint: Think about the difference between 'need' and 'want.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the story?
options: Old things should always be thrown away
A thing can matter even when no one needs it
Fishermen do not like the sea
questions.explanation: The last line: 'Sometimes a thing can matter even when no one needs it.'

questions.prompt: What does Pedro do every morning?
How does the fishermen's letter-writing show their feelings?
options: They cared about the light enough to act
They wanted to leave the island
They did not care at all
questions.explanation: Writing 'many letters' to keep the light shows they cared.
questions.hint: Think about why people write letters to the government.

So the light still shines. Pedro is happy. He cleans the glass every morning. He paints the white walls in summer. The lighthouse is not useful, but it is {{b11}}. Sometimes a thing can {{b12}} even when no one needs it.

blanks.explanation: Pedro looks after the lighthouse, so he is the keeper.
He turns on the light 'every night.'
Ships 'do not need' his light now.
The light is for the fishermen.
When they see the light they feel calm.
The government wanted to close it.
They said it was old and useless.
A thing can 'matter' even when no one needs it.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The fisherman said, 'We do not need it, but we want it.' Can you think of an old thing in your life that you do not need but still want to keep? Why does it matter to you?
